title: Default and Direct Exchanges, Work Queues, and Fair Dispatch · Compiler Case: Competing Consumer Semantics
summary: A task service publishes through the default exchange without naming the coupling, assumes one queue broadcasts, uses auto-ack workers, and calls round-robin dispatch fair despite unequal task cost. Planned artifact: Add and verify the competing consumer semantics increment in direct-routing work distribution service. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Use one queue with multiple consumers for load distribution and state that each delivery goes to one active consumer at a time.
Design direct exchange bindings that keep producer intent stable while consumer queues evolve.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: RabbitMQ and TypeScript Predict Competing Consumer Semantics · Theory Competing Consumer Semantics
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory messaging team handles case 1141 in a multi-tenant case event backbone with three subscriber responsibilities. Build deterministic TypeScript evidence to combine direct routing, worker capacity, and acknowledgement-after-effect evidence; bound payload, buffer, and retained-ledger bytes; then remove the dead-letter binding and expose the resulting retention risk. Browser code models pure messaging decisions only. amqplib, AMQP sockets, RabbitMQ, TLS, partitions, restarts, Docker, databases, external effects, load, upgrades, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Use one queue with multiple consumers for load distribution and state that each delivery goes to one active consumer at a time.

Before reading, predict which action preserves the Competing Consumer Semantics contract for the supplied data.

Evidence target: RabbitMQ and TypeScript Predict Competing Consumer Semantics · Theory Competing Consumer Semantics.
